flashcards.front: What is the fundamental principle of a Queue data structure?
flashcards.back: FIFO (First-In-First-Out): The element that enters the queue first is the first one to leave.

flashcards.front: Define the enqueue operation in a queue.
flashcards.back: Enqueue (or push) adds an element to the rear (tail) end of the queue.

flashcards.front: Define the dequeue operation in a queue.
flashcards.back: Dequeue (or pop) removes and returns the element from the front (head) of the queue.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of enqueue and dequeue operations in a queue?
flashcards.back: Both enqueue and dequeue operations have O(1) time complexity in a properly implemented queue.
A circular queue wraps the rear pointer around to the beginning of the array when it reaches the end, utilizing unused space.

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of a circular queue over a linear queue?
flashcards.back: Circular queue efficiently uses space by reusing empty slots after dequeue, whereas linear queue wastes space.
A deque allows insertion and deletion of elements from both the front and rear ends of the queue.
A priority queue arranges elements based on priority rather than FIFO order; highest priority element is served first.

flashcards.front: What are the two main implementations of a queue?
What is overflow condition in a queue?
flashcards.back: Overflow occurs when we try to insert an element in a full queue (in array-based implementation with fixed size).

flashcards.front: What is underflow condition in a queue?
flashcards.back: Underflow occurs when we try to delete an element from an empty queue.

flashcards.front: What are the applications of queues in computing?
flashcards.back: CPU scheduling, disk scheduling, BFS traversal of graphs/trees, print job spooling, message queues, and asynchronous data transfer.

flashcards.front: Front points to the element to be removed next (first element), rear points to the last element inserted.
How is a linked list-based queue better than array-based?
flashcards.back: Linked list implementation has no size limitation (dynamic) and doesn't waste memory, while array-based has fixed capacity.
Think of a ticket counter line: people join at the back (rear) and get served from the front (head).
